meta.title: Evoque Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Evoque Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every generation. Every engine. Every known failure. Every replacement cost — including the timing-chain question that hangs over the used market, answered honestly. Backed by 741+ real UK Evoque enquiries [EM-VERIFIED].

trustStrip.label: 741+ Enquiries (2025) — #1 Model Family in Our Data

★★★☆☆ — the familiar Ingenium arc: caveated pre-2019, genuinely solid after
2020+ L551 D165 or P200 — post-improvement engines at post-depreciation prices
L551 P200 — the lowest-caveat entry into the badge, full stop
🥇 #1 Model Family by Enquiry Volume — 741 enquiries (2025) [EM-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Evoque is most people's first Range Rover — which is exactly why we're blunt about it: the difference between a great first Range Rover and a cautionary tale is one facelift year and one £250 chain inspection.

generations.subHeadline: Two generations — and inside the first one, two very different engine eras. Select yours.

cards.title: Evoque L538
cards.verdict: The car that made Range Rover a fashion brand. Early 2.2 diesels are honest and well understood; 2015–2018 Ingenium diesels are fine engines wearing the chain question — inspection paperwork decides the deal.

cards.title: Evoque L551
cards.verdict: The grown-up Evoque — post-improvement Ingenium engines from day one, mild-hybrid drivetrains, and the P300e for urban lives. The lowest-caveat first Range Rover money buys.

cards.title: The Line That Matters: 2019
cards.meta: Every L551 was built after the Ingenium chain-hardware improvements — the caveat that follows the L538's 2015–2018 diesels simply doesn't apply. If you're comparing a £13,000 2017 L538 against a £17,000 2019 L551, the £4,000 gap is buying you more than a newer badge: it's buying out the inspection anxiety entirely.

comparisonLink.label: Read the full comparison: Evoque L538 vs L551 →

engineDatabase.h2: Evoque Engine Codes — The Complete Database

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: 204DTD and 204PT ratings: 95% confidence — the deepest evidence base on the site: 1,150 JLR-wide 204DTD enquiries plus 741 model-level Evoque enquiries across three cohorts [EM-VERIFIED]. The P300e's 148-enquiry cohort is the largest PHEV dataset we hold, though the parc is young — rated Provisional-plus. 224DT and Si4: Expert-knowledge tier. Data note (internal): Source sheet corrections: (1) added the Si4 GTDi petrol (2011–2017) the sheet omitted — petrol L538s exist and their owners need a row; (2) 224DT years tightened to 2011–2015 (replaced by Ingenium at the 2015 facelift); (3) added the AJ20P3 P300e as its own engine — the sheet's "204PT" grouping would misroute PHEV owners, and the 148-enquiry PHEV cohort [EM-VERIFIED] deserves its own engine page.

problems.description: The Evoque's defining used-market question and our #1 enquiry driver on this model [EM-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle is the warning. Applies to 2015–2018 L538 diesels; L551s carry the improved hardware. £3,500–£5,800 recon [EM-QUOTE].
The urban-Evoque problem: short school-run journeys interrupt DPF regens, fuel dilutes the oil, wear accelerates. If your Evoque never sees a motorway, you want the P200 or P300e.
The early L538 diesel's known weak points — well understood after a decade of Freelander 2 and Evoque service life, and moderately priced to fix.
Coolant loss and rough running on higher-mileage Ingenium diesels — repairable if caught before overheating threatens the head.
Not an engine failure, but the L538's most common non-engine enquiry — power transfer unit wear presenting as driveline vibration, shared with its Discovery Sport sibling.
The Ford-derived petrol's age-related weak points — a specialist check item on any surviving 2011–2017 petrol L538.

marketIntelligence.h2: Evoque Market Intelligence — What 741 UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising as the 2019–2020 parc exits warranty [PLACEHOLDER]

signals.data: 148 [EM-VERIFIED] — the largest PHEV cohort we track

insights: Interesting insight: The Evoque family generates more engine enquiries than any other model line we track — 741 in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED] — yet its typical repair bill is among the smallest. Britain's biggest engine-enquiry model isn't the one with the worst engines; it's the one with the most first-time premium owners meeting their first warning light.

The 148-enquiry PHEV cohort [EM-VERIFIED] is the earliest large-scale signal anyone in the UK has on plug-in hybrid engine longevity — and so far it skews toward charging and software questions, not mechanical failures. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)
L538 enquiries cluster on 2015–2017 diesels around 70,000–100,000 miles — the chain-anxiety zone, arriving exactly on schedule. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Evoque Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one Evoque based on this page, buy a 2020+ L551. And if the budget says 2015–2018 diesel, spend £250 on a chain inspection before you spend £13,000 on the car — it's the best-value insurance in this entire segment.

replacementCosts.h2: Evoque Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.figuresNote: 204DTD and 204PT figures [EM-QUOTE]; 224DT, Si4 and AJ20P3 rows [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs Table A].
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: 10–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE]. The Evoque rule — the generation split: Every L551 clears our 60% scrap-threshold rule with room to spare — ✅ Safe. The pressure sits on the cheapest L538s: a £5,800 worst-case recon against a £7,500 2015 diesel is 77% — ❌ past the line, and a good used engine (£2,000–£3,500) or walking away becomes the honest answer. On a £12,000+ L538, replacement is back in play. Two model years and £4,000 of value separate 'always fix it' from 'run the maths first.'

engineEvolution.h2: How the Evoque Engine Range Evolved — 2011 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: The Evoque launched Range Rover into the compact-premium market on proven Ford-era engines
eras.worthKnowing: It became JLR's fastest-selling car ever — this parc is why Evoque enquiries lead our data today

eras.whyBmwChanged: The 2015 facelift brought JLR's new in-house engines — and the early chain hardware with them
eras.worthKnowing: The 2016–2018 Convertible shares these engines; same advice, fewer roofs

eras.whyBmwChanged: A new platform built for electrification — post-improvement engines from day one, PHEV from 2020
eras.worthKnowing: The first Evoque generation with no engine caveat at launch

profiles.verdict: This is the Evoque's home turf — the most approachable route into the badge, with every known issue documented and priced. Start with a 2020+ P200 and it's a genuinely easy first Range Rover.
Stylish and safe, but compact — fine for one child, tight for two with kit. If boot space is a weekly argument, the Discovery Sport is the same money and mechanicals with more car.
The P300e is the best urban-commuter Range Rover made — 30+ electric miles covers most British commutes. Diesel Evoques belong on motorway commutes only.
1,600–2,000kg braked — a small trailer, not a caravan habit. Buyers with towing ambitions should read our Discovery Sport page instead.
Early 2.2 L538s are honest cheap entries; 2015–2018 Ingenium diesels are the caveat zone — inspection first, always. Never spend recon money on a sub-£8,000 car without running our maths.

items.question: How much does an Evoque engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Evoque engine typically costs £2,800–£5,800 supply only [EM-QUOTE], with the volume 204DTD diesel at £3,500–£5,800. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Evoque engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The 204PT petrol and post-2019 204DTD diesels — the L551 carries the improved Ingenium hardware from day one. Early P300e evidence is strong too, on the largest PHEV dataset in the UK [EM-VERIFIED].

items.question: Which Evoque engine should I avoid?
items.answer: None outright — but a 2015–2018 Ingenium diesel without a documented timing-chain inspection is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is fine; the unknown is not.

items.question: Do Evoques have timing chain problems?
items.answer: The 2015–2018 L538 Ingenium diesels carry the known tensioner-wear caveat — cold-start rattle is the warning. Every L551 (2019+) was built after the hardware improvements. This distinction is the single most important fact in the used Evoque market. Hearing the rattle? Use our diagnostic calculator.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an Evoque engine?
items.answer: On any L551 — yes, comfortably within our 60% scrap-threshold rule. On L538s it's value-dependent: above ~£10,000, usually yes; on the cheapest cars a used engine (£1,500–£3,500) or walking away is the honest answer. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.

items.question: Which Evoque should I buy?
items.answer: A 2020-or-later L551 — P200 for town, D180/D200 for motorway miles, P300e for short-run urban lives. Among L538s, an early 2.2 with history or a 2015+ diesel with chain paperwork.

items.question: How do I find my Evoque engine code?
items.answer: On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The year does most of the work: 2.2 badges to 2015 = 224DT; 2.0 diesels from 2015 = 204DTD; P300e = the 1.5 three-cylinder AJ20P3. Or tell us your variant and year and we'll identify it.

items.question: Evoque vs Discovery Sport — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Mechanically they're siblings — same platform family, same engines, same pre/post-2019 story. The Discovery Sport adds the 5+2 seats; the Evoque adds the style. Buy on year and history, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
